1: homothymadon (homothymadon, 'with one accord/purpose') appears eleven times in Acts and only once elsewhere in the NT (Rom 15:6). It is not merely spatial togetherness but a deliberate unity of will and disposition — the kind of communal harmony that Ps 133:1 calls 'good and pleasant'. Luke signals that Pentecost descends on a community already in relational order.
4: heterais glossais (heterais glōssais, 'other/different tongues') identifies actual foreign languages — confirmed by the catalogue in vv. 9–11 of identifiable nations who each hear 'in our own tongue' (tē hēmeterā dialektō). This is distinct from the ecstatic glossolalia of 1 Cor 14. The Pentecost miracle partially reverses Babel (Gen 11:1–9): where Babel dispersed by language confusion, the Spirit unifies across language boundaries. apophthengesthai (apophtheggesthai, 'to utter', 2:4, 14) is a semi-technical term for inspired prophetic speech; used of the Delphic oracle and in LXX for prophetic utterance (Mic 5:12; Ezek 13:9).
38: metanoeite (metanoeite) is the same imperative John and Jesus used (Mark 1:15; Luke 13:3,5); here it opens the community's first public invitation. baptistheto ... eis aphesin hamartion: the preposition eis marks direction or purpose — baptism toward/for forgiveness of sins, not a meritorious cause. dorea (dōrea, 'gift') marks the Spirit as freely bestowed, not earned; the same word for God's indescribable gift in 2 Cor 9:15. The sequence metanoia → baptism → forgiveness → Spirit-gift became the pattern by which Acts measures conversion.
42: te didache ton apostolon ... te koinonia ... te klasei tou artou ... tais proseuchais: Luke lists four activities of the Jerusalem community in asyndeton (no conjunctions), giving them equal structural weight. koinonia (koinōnia) is not merely 'fellowship' but participatory sharing — of goods (v. 44–45), of the Lord's Supper, of the Spirit (2 Cor 13:14). klasis tou artou is a Semitic phrase for breaking bread at meals; in Luke 24:35 it was the Emmaus recognition moment, suggesting eucharistic weight here.
2: ēchos hōsper pheromenēs pnoēs biaias (ēchos hōsper pheromenēs pnoēs biaias, 'a sound like a rushing violent wind'): pnoē (wind/breath) echoes the creation-breath of Gen 2:7 (pnoēn zōēs) and Ezek 37:9's life-giving wind; biaios (violent/forceful) underscores that this is not gentle inspiration but overwhelming divine power. The sound fills the whole house before any visible sign appears — the Spirit is first heard, then seen.
3: diamerizomenai glōssai hōsei pyros (diamerizomenai glōssai hōsei pyros, 'tongues distributing/dividing like fire'): the fire distributes to individuals — diamerizomenai indicates division into separate portions, one per person. The fire resting on each person recalls the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:18) and Elijah's fire-baptism prediction (Luke 3:16), but the individual distribution is new: the divine presence is no longer centralized in the temple but personally imparted to each believer.
5: Ioudaioi eulabeis apo pantos ethnous ton hypo ton ouranon (Ioudaioi eulabeis apo pantos ethnous tōn hypo ton ouranon, 'God-fearing Jews from every nation under heaven'): eulabeis (devout/reverent) is a characteristic Lukan virtue-word (Simeon, Luke 2:25; Cornelius, Acts 10:2). The diaspora Jews' presence in Jerusalem for Pentecost explains why representatives of so many nations could be present simultaneously; they are not Gentile God-fearers but diaspora Jews whose languages reflect their dispersion.
6: synēlthe to plēthos kai synechy the ('the crowd came together and was bewildered'): sygchyō (bewildered/confused) is the same verb as Gen 11:9 LXX for the Babel confusion (synecheen). Luke may intend the etymological echo: what God confused at Babel the Spirit now unconfuses. Each person hears in their own dialect (tē idia dialektō) — the miracle is not the speaking but the hearing-in-comprehension.
7: exīstanto de kai ethaumazon (existanto de kai ethaumazon, 'they were astonished and amazed'): Luke uses the imperfect — the astonishment was ongoing as they kept hearing. hoi Galilaioi (the Galileans): the audience's identification of the speakers as Galileans sets up the social irony Luke has prepared since 1:11 — the provincial uneducated disciples are the vehicles of the Spirit's work that crosses all linguistic and ethnic boundaries.
8: hēmeterā dialektō en hē egennēthēmen (hēmeterā dialektō en hē egennēthēmen, 'our own dialect in which we were born'): dialektos is specifically regional dialect, not merely language; the miracle reaches to the phonological particularity of each person's birth-tongue. The emphasis on birth-tongue rather than learned language intensifies the personal character of the Pentecost reversal — the Spirit meets each person in their most intimate communicative medium.
9: Parthoi kai Mēdoi kai Elamitai: the catalog moves roughly from east (Parthians, Medes, Elamites — Mesopotamia) through the Levant (Judea, Cappadocia, Pontus, Asia) to the diaspora West. The list is not exhaustive but representative; scholars have proposed various structural principles (zodiac signs, Roman administrative regions) none of which fully accounts for it. The theological point is the comprehensiveness: pan ethnous (every nation) is being demonstrated concretely.
10: proselytos (prosēlytos, 'proselyte/convert'): the inclusion of proselytes alongside Jews in the crowd's self-description signals that the new community will include both ethnic Jews and those who have formally joined Israel. The Roman visitors (both Ioudaioi te kai prosēlytoi) anticipate the Cornelius episode (ch.10) where the Roman Gentile connection will become central.
11: ta megaleia tou theou (ta megaleia tou theou, 'the great works of God'): the content of the Spirit-inspired speech is not glossolalic ecstasy but intelligible proclamation of God's great deeds — the same content as OT hymns (Ps 71:19; 96:3; Luke 1:49). The Pentecost miracle is fundamentally about comprehensible communication of the divine acts, not private spiritual experience.
12: diēporoun (diēporoun, 'they were perplexed/at a loss'): diaporeō (be completely at a loss) is a strong term — not mere puzzlement but genuine cognitive inability to categorize the event. The question ti thelei touto einai ('what does this mean?') is a genuinely open interpretive question; Peter's speech (v.14ff.) will provide the answer that resolves the diaporia.
13: gleukous memestōmenoi eisin (gleukous memestōmenoi eisin, 'they are full of sweet new wine'): gleukos is fresh grape juice, not necessarily fermented wine; the charge may be that they are intoxicated by the excitement of the new vintage festival. The accusation provides the foil for Peter's counterargument (v.15: 'it's only nine in the morning') — too early for drunkenness even by ancient standards.
14: statheis de ho Petros syn tois hendeka epēren tēn phōnen (statheis de ho Petros syn tois hendeka epēren tēn phōnēn, 'Peter standing with the Eleven raised his voice'): the verb statheis (stood) marks the beginning of formal public address. The Eleven are in solidarity — Peter speaks as representative, not soloist. Epēren tēn phōnēn (raised his voice) is the oratorical gesture of formal speech; Peter claims the public forum of the gathered diaspora crowd.
15: hē trite hōra (hē tritē hōra, 'the third hour'): 9 a.m. by Roman reckoning; the first of the three Jewish prayer hours (Ps 55:17; Dan 6:10), when public drinking would not have begun. Peter's argument is pragmatic — the observable sobriety of 120 people excludes the drunkenness charge. The specific hour grounds the narrative in ordinary public time, not private visionary experience.
16: touto estin to eiremenon dia tou prophētou Iōēl (touto estin to eirēmenon dia tou prophētou Iōēl, 'this is what was spoken through the prophet Joel'): touto estin ('this is') is the pesher formula — the observed phenomenon is identified as the fulfillment of the scriptural prediction. Peter does not say Joel predicted Pentecost; he says Pentecost is what Joel predicted. The interpretation moves from event to text, not text to prediction.
17: en tais eschatais hemerais (en tais eschatais hēmerais, 'in the last days'): Peter changes Joel's 'after these things' (meta tauta) to 'in the last days' (en tais eschatais hēmerais), making the eschatological orientation explicit — Pentecost inaugurates the last days. The Spirit's outpouring on pasan sarka ('all flesh') breaks the previous restriction of prophecy to designated prophets and designated times.
18: kai ge epi tous doulous mou kai epi tas doulas mou (kai ge epi tous doulous mou kai epi tas doulas mou, 'even on my male and female slaves/servants'): the lowest social category receives the Spirit equally. The kai ge (even!) emphasizes the unexpected inclusion; the slave has no social standing and yet receives the identical Spirit-gift as the free citizen. The Spirit's gift inverts social hierarchies without abolishing them.
19: terata kai semeia (terata kai sēmeia, 'wonders and signs'): Joel's cosmic portents are not necessarily literal but belong to the apocalyptic register for eschatological disruption (Isa 13:10; Ezek 32:7-8). Peter's Joel-citation extends the fulfillment claim beyond Pentecost to the whole last-days era, within which the signs continue. Luke does not stop to explain how much of Joel is fulfilled at Pentecost vs. deferred to the parousia — the category is inaugurated eschatology.
20: hēmera kyriou tēn megalēn kai epiphanē (hēmera kyriou tēn megalēn kai epiphanē, 'the great and manifest day of the Lord'): the LXX's epiphanē (manifest/glorious) adds the public-display dimension to Joel's 'great and terrible' (gadol wenora). The day of the Lord is simultaneously glorious (for those who call on the name) and terrible (for those who do not) — Peter's sermon is urgently evangelistic because the day has been inaugurated.
21: pas hos an epikalesētai to onoma kyriou sōthēsetai (pas hos an epikalesētai to onoma kyriou sōthēsetai, 'everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved'): the pas (everyone) and the onoma kyriou (name of the Lord) are the theological hinges. Joel 2:32 in the MT uses YHWH; Peter's audience hears kyrios = YHWH, and v.36 will identify Jesus as Kyrios. The Joel citation thus sets up the Christological identification that concludes the sermon.
22: apodedeigmenon apo tou theou (apodedeigmenon apo tou theou, 'having been demonstrated/accredited by God'): apodeiknymi is the term for proving something publicly through evidence — the miracles were God's letter of recommendation for Jesus. dynamesin kai terasin kai sēmeiois (powers, wonders, and signs): the three-fold designation for authenticating signs; the same trio used for the apostles' ministry (2:43; 5:12) and Paul's (2 Cor 12:12).
23: tē hōrismenē boulē kai prognōsei tou theou (tē hōrismenē boulē kai prognōsei tou theou, 'the determined plan and foreknowledge of God'): horizō (determine, set the boundary) is strong — not merely foreknown but decreed. Boulē (counsel/plan) and prognōsis (foreknowledge) cover both the eternal intention and its temporal anticipation. The cross is neither an accident nor a coup; it is the execution of the divine counsel. The human agency (you crucified him) is fully real alongside the divine necessity — Luke does not soften either side.
24: adynaton ēn krateisthai auton hyp' autou (adynaton ēn krateisthai auton hyp' autou, 'it was impossible for him to be held by it'): adynatos (impossible) is a strong word — death had no capacity to retain the author of life. The grounds of the impossibility are implicit: God cannot be defeated by the creature he made; the one who created life cannot be ended by death permanently. The resurrection is not a surprise reversal but a logical necessity given who Jesus is.
25: proōrōmēn ton kyrion enōpion mou dia pantos (proōrōmēn ton kyrion enōpion mou dia pantos, 'I saw the Lord always before me'): Peter quotes Ps 16:8-11 as prophecy about Christ. Proōraō (see beforehand/set before oneself) can mean either to foresee or to keep in constant view; both meanings are at play. The Psalm of unshakeable confidence in YHWH's presence even at death is attributed to Christ — David speaks in Christ's person.
26: hē sarx mou kataskēnōsei ep' elpidi (hē sarx mou kataskēnōsei ep' elpidi, 'my flesh will dwell in hope'): the sarx (flesh/body) dwelling in hope is bodily resurrection expectation, not spiritual immortality. The hope is for the body's restoration — the LXX's kataskēnoō (to encamp/dwell) evokes the tabernacling presence of God. The body that hopes is the body that will be raised.
27: ou enkatalipseis tēn psychen mou eis hadou (ou enkatalipseis tēn psychēn mou eis Hadou, 'you will not abandon my soul to Hades'): Peter's exegetical argument (vv.29-31) rests on this verse: David's tomb is present to this day (v.29), so the hosios (holy one) who will not see corruption cannot be David himself. The argument is Rabbinic in form but pneumatological in ground: David spoke 'prophetically' about one other than himself.
28: plērophoreias meta tou prosōpou sou (plērōseis me euphrosynēs meta tou prosōpou sou, 'you will fill me with gladness in your presence'): plēroō (fill) applied to the joy of resurrection-presence; the fullness of eschatological joy is the goal of the paschal journey. The verse is the climax of Peter's Psalm 16 citation, pointing beyond resurrection to the permanent joy of the ascended Christ in the Father's presence.
29: exon eipein meta parrēsias (exon eipein meta parrēsias, 'it is possible to speak freely/boldly'): parrēsia (free speech, boldness) is one of Acts' key words for the apostolic proclamation — used for Peter before the Sanhedrin (4:13), Paul's final preaching (28:31), and the church's prayer (4:29). Peter's bold claim about David's tomb is the rhetorical pivot for the argument: he appeals to a fact his audience can verify.
30: prophētēs hyparchōn kai eidōs (prophētēs hyparchōn kai eidōs, 'being a prophet and knowing'): prophetic knowledge is what grounds David's messianic Psalms — not merely poetic inspiration but specific divine disclosure. The oath God swore (hōmosen autō ho theos, citing Ps 132:11 / 2 Sam 7:12-13) was known to David as binding covenant commitment; David's prophetic knowledge of the resurrection flows from his knowledge of the unbreakable Davidic covenant.
31: proïdōn elalēsen peri tēs anastaseōs tou Christou (proïdōn elalēsen peri tēs anastaseōs tou Christou, 'seeing this beforehand he spoke of the resurrection of the Christ'): Peter's interpretive move is decisive: Ps 16 is about the resurrection, and the Christ is its subject. Proïdōn (having foreseen/seen beforehand) is the prophet's special sight — David does not merely hope for resurrection in general but prophesies specifically about the Messiah's resurrection.
32: martyres pantes hēmeis esmen (martyres pantes hēmeis esmen, 'witnesses of this are we all'): the plural and the inclusive pantes (all) claim eyewitness status for the whole Eleven, not merely Peter. The resurrection witness is the apostolic foundation claim; its validity rests on multiple converging witnesses. The assertion closes the Psalm 16 argument with an empirical declaration: the text predicted it; we saw it.
33: hypsōtheis tē dexia tou theou (hypsōtheis tē dexia tou theou, 'having been exalted at the right hand of God'): the ascension-enthronement completes the resurrection-argument. Hypsoomai (be exalted) was Jesus's own term for the cross-and-resurrection (John 3:14; 12:32); now it applies to the ascension. The exalted Christ has received the Spirit from the Father — the Spirit poured out at Pentecost is evidence of the completed ascension, not a separate event.
34: ou gar David anebē eis tous ouranous (ou gar David anebē eis tous ouranous, 'for David did not ascend into the heavens'): the negative argument from Ps 110:1 mirrors the negative argument from Ps 16 (David's tomb is present = he did not rise). The two Psalms form Peter's double-proof: Psalm 16 establishes resurrection; Psalm 110 establishes enthronement. Together they are the OT ground for 'God has made this Jesus both Lord and Messiah.'
35: heos an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou (heōs an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou, 'until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet'): the Ps 110:1 citation includes the temporal qualifier heōs (until) — the enthronement is current and will remain until the subjugation of enemies is complete. This generates the NT's inaugurated eschatology: Christ is enthroned now, the enemies are being subdued progressively, and the final submission awaits the parousia.
36: Kyrion kai Christon epoiēsen ho theos touton ton Iēsoun (Kyrion kai Christon epoiēsen ho theos touton ton Iēsoun, 'God has made this Jesus both Lord and Messiah'): the climax of Peter's sermon and Acts' first Christological affirmation. Kyrion (Lord) applied to Jesus while citing kyrios in Ps 110 LXX (= YHWH) implies the highest Christological identification. Epoiēsen (made): this is not eternal ontology but the declaration of what the resurrection-enthronement has confirmed — the installed messianic king is the YHWH-Lord of the Psalm.
37: katenygēsan tēn kardian (katenygēsan tēn kardian, 'they were cut to the heart'): katanyssō (pierce/stab) appears only here in the NT; in LXX it can describe sharp grief (Gen 34:7; Ps 109:16). The crowd's response is not curiosity but compunction — genuine recognition that they stand in the wrong. The question ti poiēsōmen ('what shall we do?') is the right response to conviction; Peter's answer (v.38) addresses it directly.
39: pasin tois eis makran (pasin tois eis makran, 'all who are far off'): a phrase with OT background (Isa 57:19; cf. Acts 22:21) that will become technical for Gentile inclusion (Eph 2:13,17). At this stage Peter may mean diaspora Jews; the full Gentile implication is not reached until Acts 10. hosous an proskalesētai kyrios ho theos ('as many as the Lord our God will call'): divine calling precedes and grounds human response.
40: tēs geneas tēs skoleias tautes (tēs geneas tēs skoliās tautēs, 'this crooked generation'): skolias (crooked, perverse) echoes Deut 32:5's Song of Moses ('a perverse and crooked generation') applied to faithless Israel. Peter uses Moses's own language for covenantal apostasy to describe the generation that rejected and crucified Jesus; repentance and baptism are the exodus from that generation into the new covenant community.
41: prose tethēsan tē hēmera ekeinē psychai hōsei trischiliai (prosetethe tē hēmerā ekeinē psychai hōsei trischiliai, 'there were added that day about three thousand persons'): prosethēsan (were added) is the divine passive — God adds to the community (v.47 confirms: 'the Lord added'). Psychai (souls/persons) counts individuals. The contrast with Sinai's three thousand dead (Exod 32:28) on the day the Law was given is the kind of intertextual irony Luke crafts throughout Acts.
43: egineto de pase psyche phobos (egineto de pasē psychē phobos, 'awe came on every soul'): phobos (fear/awe) in Luke-Acts is typically the response to divine presence and divine power (Luke 1:12, 65; 2:9; 5:26; 7:16; Acts 5:5, 11). The wonders and signs performed through the apostles are the continuation of Jesus's miracle-ministry; the apostolic authority is not self-generated but derived from the risen Christ. terata kai sēmeia: the signs are not ends in themselves but witnesses that confirm the resurrection proclamation.
44: pantes de hoi pisteuontes ēsan epi to auto kai eichon hapanta koina (pantes de hoi pisteuontes ēsan epi to auto kai eichon hapanta koina, 'all the believers were together and had all things in common'): epi to auto ('in the same place') is a phrase that recurs in Acts for community assembly (1:15; 2:1, 44, 47). Koina (common/shared) is the Hellenistic ideal of true friendship; Luke presents the Jerusalem church as the community where the friendship-ideal is actually achieved — not communism by mandate but generosity by Spirit-prompted love.
45: epipraskōn ktēmata kai hyparcheis (epipraskon ktēmata kai hyparcheis, 'they were selling possessions and property'): the imperfects (epipraskōn, diemerization) indicate ongoing, repeated action as needs arose — not a single distribution but a habitual practice. Acts 5:4 confirms that ownership was retained; the selling was voluntary and ad hoc. The pastoral ideal here is the complete availability of property to meet need, not the abolition of private ownership.
46: kath' hēmeran te proskarterountes homothymadon en tō hierō (kath' hēmeran te proskarterountes homothymadon en tō hierō, 'daily continuing with one accord in the temple'): proskartereō (continue steadfastly, devote oneself to) is one of Luke's characteristic discipleship verbs (1:14; 2:42). The community maintains both temple (public Jewish worship) and house-table (private Eucharistic fellowship) — not yet separated from Judaism but already distinctively new. agoalliasis (agalliasis, exultant joy): the eschatological joy of those who know the Messiah has come and the Spirit has arrived.
47: echontes charin pros holon ton laon (echontes charin pros holon ton laon, 'having favor with all the people'): charis (grace/favor) here means social goodwill — the community's generosity, worship life, and miracle-attestation created favorable public impression. ho de kyrios prosetithei tous sōzomenous ('the Lord added those who were being saved'): the divine passive continues — salvation and addition to the community is the Lord's act, not merely the apostles'. The imperfect prosetithei (was adding) indicates continuous daily growth.
